At least two injured in Russian attack on dwellings, school in Ukraine's Kharkiv
data.description:
Jan 26 (Reuters) - Russian forces on Monday launched a drone and missile attack on Kharkiv, Ukraine's second-largest city, hitting apartment buildings, a school and a kindergarten and injuring at least two people, Mayor Ihor Terekhov said on Telegram.

Unofficial Telegram channels posted pictures showing the city, a frequent Russian target located 30 km (18 miles) from the Russian border, had been plunged into darkness.

In Kryvyi Rih, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy's home town to the southeast of Kharkiv, the head of the industrial city's military administration said Russian drones hit a high-rise apartment building.

Oleksandr Vilkul, writing on Telegram, said the impact had triggered a fire, but gave no immediate indication of any casualties.

Reuters could not independently verify the reports and there was no immediate reaction from Russian officials.

(Reporting by Ron Popeski; Editing by Kevin Liffey and Paul Simao)

Trump's Tariff Threats Designed to Influence Pending USMCA Talks, Canada PM Carney Says -- 2nd Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira


OTTAWA--Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said President Trump's tariff threats likely represent some prepositioning ahead of negotiations later this year to renew the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade pact.

Carney said Monday he expects a robust review of the continental trade pact, known as USMCA, which the Trump administration will lead later this year. Trade analysts expect Trump and his negotiators to push for more concessions from Canada to keep the deal intact.

Trade tension between the U.S.-Canada accelerated over the past week, with Trump and key administration officials criticizing Carney for striking a trade detente with China, America's biggest rival; and for delivering remarks at the World Economic Forum largely viewed as a rebuke of the White House's aggressive bid to use economic and military might for geopolitical goals.

Trump threatened on Saturday 100% tariffs on all Canadian imports should Ottawa and Beijing strike a free-trade pact. Carney said Canada and China moved to resolve a specific tariff row dealing with Chinese electric vehicles and Canadian agricultural and food products, and Ottawa has no intention of pursuing a broad free-trade pact with Beijing.

"I think some of these comments and positioning should be viewed in the broader context" of the pending USMCA review, Carney said at an event to unveil tax-relief measures. He added Canada and the U.S. have a "remarkable economic and security relationship, but that can be improved, and that is something that can be dealt with in negotiations."

Brian Clow, a senior aide to former Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, said he expects officials in Ottawa to try to de-escalate tensions with their U.S. counterparts in the next few weeks. "This government knows there are always ups and downs with Trump and they have the discipline to stay calm and focused," said Clow, who advised Trudeau on U.S.-Canada relations.

Eurasia Group, the geopolitical risk firm, said it expects the Trump administration to use Canada's decision to reduce tariffs on some China-made EVs as leverage to extract further concessions. The Canada-China agreement calls for China to send about 49,000 EVs to Canada at a tariff rate of about 6%, well below the 100% duty that Canada imposed in 2024 to align China policy with the U.S.

"If Washington treats the EV carve?out as a precedent, expect harder lines from the U.S. on autos and batteries, a stricter views on subsidies, and broader tariffs floated as leverage," said Kevin Ford, a foreign-exchange and macro strategist at Convera, a cross-border payments and currency exchange firm.

Canada's economy is struggling under the weight of U.S. tariffs of up to 50% on sectors such as steel, aluminum, motor vehicles and forest products. Still, about 80% of Canadian exports to the U.S. are entering duty free because they comply with the terms in USMCA. The USMCA talks represent an elevated source of uncertainty, officials and economists say, leading to businesses curtailing investment and hiring plans, and increased worries among households about job security.

The Bank of Canada issues its first policy decision of 2026 on Wednesday, and is expected to keep its main interest rate unchanged at 2.25%. Economists, though, wonder whether the central bank will cast further doubt about growth given the trade environment.

Carney has made trade diversification a priority policy matter, to reduce dependence on a protectionist U.S. His speech last week in Davos, Switzerland, warned attendees that the Western-led, rules-based trading system is history, and that smaller powers like Canada need to work collectively to counter economic coercion from global hegemons. Carney didn't identify Trump in his remarks.

Carney told reporters Monday he has no regrets about his speech, even though Trump and other senior officials weren't impressed. Trump, in his Davos speech, said Canada "gets a lot of freebies from us," and that, "Canada lives because of the U.S."

"I described the current situation. You have to be honest," Carney said. "And that highlights the need to diversify our trading relationships."


Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-26-26 1357ET

European countries including UK lose measles elimination status
data.description:
LONDON, Jan 26 (Reuters) - Britain and several other European countries have lost their measles elimination status, the World Health Organization said on Monday, after a jump in infections across the continent.

Spain, Austria, Armenia, Azerbaijan and Uzbekistan also lost their status, and the WHO urged countries to boost vaccination rates, particularly among under-protected populations, to prevent the viral disease infecting more children. 

Measles is entirely preventable by vaccination, but is very contagious, and so is among the first illnesses to rebound when vaccination rates decline. It commonly causes symptoms including high fever and a rash, but can also lead to serious long-term complications and even death.

SIGN OF DECLINING VACCINATION 

Health experts have warned that rising outbreaks worldwide point to a resurgence of other preventable illnesses in populations increasingly mistrustful or skeptical of vaccines since the COVID-19 pandemic. 

"The UK's change of status reflects a broader challenge we're facing across the WHO European Region," the U.N. health agency said on its website. Several other European countries already have regular measles transmission, according to the WHO, including France and Romania. 

Canada lost its elimination status last year, and the U.S. is working to retain its status as infections mount.

WHO committees in each region decide whether a country is measles-free using case rates. To be considered measles-free by the WHO, a country must have no locally transmitted cases of the same strain for 12 months or longer. 

The decision to strip certain European countries of their status was made last September based on data from 2024. But the WHO only released the information on Monday after getting sign-off from every country involved.

To keep measles at bay, vaccination rates must exceed 95%, the WHO estimates. Yet in the UK, only 84.4% of children had the two doses needed for full protection in 2024. Government data showed 2,911 confirmed cases in England that year, the most since 2012.   

On Monday, the UK Health Security Agency said all children should be vaccinated to protect themselves from measles. The UK was first given elimination status in 2016, before losing it in 2018 then regaining it in 2021.

(Reporting by Jennifer Rigby and Muvija M; Editing by Kevin Liffey)

Telesat, Hanwha Systems to Collaborate on Satellite Solutions for Canada, South Korea
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall


Telesat and a unit of the Hanwha Group are collaborating to build satellite connectivity solutions and user terminals for Telesat's Low Earth Orbit network.

The Ottawa company and Hanwha Systems signed a memorandum of understanding to work on the project, they said Monday. The MOU follows a letter of intent from the Canadian and South Korean governments to purchase LEO satellite communications and advanced maritime platforms.

The companies said they would collaborate on the Canadian Patrol Submarine Project and LEO communications-satellite constellation and defense-user terminals for the Korean government. Hanwha also said it would try to implement Telesat's Lightspeed services to its own weapons systems.

"This collaboration brings together complementary resources and expertise to deliver next--generation, sovereign LEO communications capabilities for Canada, South Korea, and our allied partners," Telesat President and Chief Executive Dan Goldberg said.


Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-26-26 1342ET

Wells Fargo, Three Others Partner on AI-Powered Solutions for Wildfires
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall


Wells Fargo, Salesforce, Lockheed Martin and PG&E are partnering to launch a new artificial-intelligence-powered wildfire solutions venture.

The new venture, known as Emberpoint, will use AI and autonomous systems to help detect wildfires, prevent their spread, and coordinate mitigation, the companies said Monday.

Wells Fargo is providing a capital investment for Emberpoint, which will be built in part on Salesforce's technology. Lockheed Martin is contributing prediction and detection technologies and autonomous response and suppression capabilities, while PG&E will bring wildfire mitigation experience, the companies said.

PG&E said it plans to seek regulatory approval to contribute to the venture.

The companies said the technology will be built out in the coming months, with demonstrations expected in this year.


Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-26-26 1335ET

Snohomish County Plans to Sell $78.4 Million of Revenue Bonds for Housing Project
data.description:
By Chris Wack


The Housing Authority of Snohomish County, Wash., plans to sell $78.4 million of Series 2026 revenue bonds to provide funds for its Allegro Apartments project.

Money from the sale will go to finance the purchase, rehabilitation and equipping of public property used for the essential governmental purposes comprised of a 240-unit multifamily housing rental development, according to documents posted Thursday on MuniOS.

The apartments will be owned by HASCO Allegro Lynnwood LLC, a limited liability company and a wholly owned subsidiary of the authority.

The project is an existing 240-unit multifamily housing rental development built between 2019 and 2025. There are 327 total parking spaces around five four-story residential buildings, three three-story rental buildings and a common area building.

HASCO Allegro Lynnwood will make payments to the authority from certain revenue available from the project, and from other available funds, if any.

Interest on the bonds is payable semiannually on April 1 and Oct. 1. The bonds mature April 1, 2036.Rates and yields have not yet been determined.

The authority is a public corporation created with the purpose of providing housing assistance to persons of low income, with the power to buy, construct, maintain operate and repair housing facilities. Snohomish County is located just north of Seattle.

The authority has applied for, but has not yet received, a rating for the bonds from S&P Global Ratings.

KeyBanc Capital Markets is listed as senior managers for the sale.


Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-26-26 1325ET

Alabama Gas District to Sell $675 Million in Gas Project Revenue Bonds
data.description:
By Chris Wack


The Black Belt Energy Gas District plans to sell $675 million of Series 2026 B gas project revenue bonds to prepay the purchase of natural gas.

The gas to be purchased will be over a 30-year period, under a prepaid natural-gas sales agreement between Aron Energy Prepay 59 LLC and Black Belt, according to documents posted on MuniOS.

Aron Energy Prepay 59 is a Delaware limited liability company, and will be obligated to deliver specified daily amounts of gas, make payments on any gas not delivered, and pay a termination payment on any early termination payment date established under the agreement.

Aron Energy will deposit proceeds from the prepayment it receives from Black Belt with Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce.

Interest on the bonds is payable semiannually on each March 1 and Sept. 1.

The bonds mature from March 1, 2027, through 2034. Rates and yields haven't yet been determined.

The bonds aren't an obligation of the state of Alabama, the member municipalities of Black Belt, or the project participants.

Black Belt is a gas district and public corporation organized by the governing bodies of Grove Hill, Jackson and Thomasville, Ala. Black Belt was formed as a joint action gas supply agency for the purpose of buying long-term natural-gas supplies for sale to Clarke-Mobile Counties Gas District.

Goldman Sachs and Stifel are listed as senior managers for the sale.


Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Dollar Weakness, Cold Weather Bullish for Prices
data.description:
By Joe Stonor


A roundup of key agricultural commodity markets for the week of Jan. 26-30 by Dow Jones Newswires in Barcelona.

GRAINS & OILSEEDS:

The conclusion of the Federal Open Market Committee's two-day meeting Wednesday will drive macro sentiment. Though market consensus is firmly behind rates being held steady--there is over a 97% probability of no change, accord to LSEG--markets will pay close attention to the comments of Chair Jerome Powell for clues on the Fed's path forward.

A suggestion that rate-cutting will resume could support agricultural commodities in the short term, as a weaker dollar is bullish for commodities. Last week's sharp drop in the dollar pushed investors towards hard assets, a trend compounded by increased geopolitical risk, analysts at Peak Trading Research wrote.

Earnings reports for major U.S. tech companies including Microsoft, Meta and Tesla--which all report Wednesday--will also weigh on broader market sentiment.

Speculators continue to bet against agricultural commodities, a dynamic which could lead to a short squeeze, in turn leading to rising prices, Peak Trading Research analysts said.

Export inspections of corn in the U.S. rose in the past week, according to data released by the Department of Agriculture. Corn inspections totaled 1.51 million metric tons for the past week--up 1.25 million tons from this time last year--though wheat inspections fell by over 100,000 on-year to 351,001 tons. Soybean inspections were up sharply on-year, with the 1.32 million tons exported for the week up from around 740,000 this time last year.

China was overwhelmingly the leading destination for U.S. soybeans shipped during the week. South Korea was the top destination for U.S. wheat, and Mexico was the leading destination for corn.

Weather in Brazil is set to remain non-threatening, though cold storms roiling mainland U.S. could drive corn prices up as transportation is complicated and ethanol costs rise. The U.S. Agriculture Department reported sub-zero temperatures across the Northern Plains at the end of last week, as market participants feared damage to crops, pushing U.S. wheat prices higher.

Wheat futures fall 1.3% in Chicago to $5.22 a bushel in European afternoon trade Tuesday, while corn futures slip 0.6% to $4.28 a bushel after rising around 1% over the weekend. Futures for Soybeans were down 0.5% at $10.62 a bushel.

SOFT COMMODITIES:

Cocoa ICE futures prices slipped 0.3% to $4,334 a ton, as the deflationary trend in the commodity continued. Cocoa prices have declined from a 2025 average of over $8,000 to below $4,500 to Jan. 23, Bernstein analysts noted.

ICE futures for coffee gained 1.6% to $35.66 a pound. Robusta coffee is in a deflationary trend, Jefferies analysts said, though Arabica nudged up slightly on-year.


Write to Joe Stonor at josephmichael.stonor@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-26-26 1321ET
